food_subgroup: Seafood noodle dish — glass noodles with prawns

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for vannamei prawn vermicelli. FOODON_00002572 (shrimp dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (crustaceans, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code for prawn vermicelli composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for vannamei prawn vermicelli dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of vannamei prawn vermicelli is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of glass noodles topped with whole vannamei prawns and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 15152) and Glass noodles, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20113) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for vannamei prawn vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and cooked mung bean glass noodles (FDC ID 169726), combined in approximate proportions of 40% prawn and 55% glass noodles by weight with 5% sauce/aromatics. Values represent a plausible composite per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mung bean glass noodles / cellophane noodles
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vannamei prawn vermicelli as a composite dish. GI of 45 (low) is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles), which have a reported GI of approximately 39–50 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. The prawn and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 43.75) / 100 ≈ 19.7, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 12.5 × 3.5 = 43.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1330 mg sodium, representing over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from glass noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of mung bean glass noodles (GI ≈ 45) produces a moderate glycemic load of approximately 19.7 per standard serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and dietary cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vannamei prawns contribute approximately 95 mg cholesterol per 100 g and the dish carries a high sodium load from condiments; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose low-sodium sauce variants where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vannamei Prawn Vermicelli is a Thai-influenced seafood noodle dish featuring whole or halved Pacific white leg prawns (Litopenaeus vannamei) served atop or combined with mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles). The dish is typically prepared by briefly steaming or stir-frying the prawns with aromatics including garlic, ginger, and spring onion, then finishing with a savoury sauce of oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and sesame oil. The glass noodles absorb the umami-rich sauce, resulting in a silky, slightly chewy texture that contrasts with the firm, sweet flesh of the vannamei prawns. Vannamei (Litopenaeus vannamei), also known as Pacific white shrimp or white leg shrimp, is the world's most commercially farmed shrimp species, prized for its mild sweetness, firm bite, and consistent quality. In Singapore, this dish is served in Thai seafood restaurants and is positioned as a premium offering due to the quality of the farmed vannamei prawns. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 8.2 g per 100 g from the prawns, and a carbohydrate base of 12.5 g per 100 g from the glass noodles. The low glycemic index of mung bean glass noodles (GI ≈ 45) makes this dish more suitable for blood glucose management than rice-based alternatives. However, the sodium content is elevated due to soy and oyster sauces, making it a moderate concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-compatible when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for vannamei prawn vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked shrimp and cooked mung bean glass noodles in approximate proportions. GI is estimated from the analogous mung bean glass noodle GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (FDC 175180) and cooked mung bean glass noodles (FDC 169726) in approximate proportions of 40% prawn, 55% glass noodles, and 5% sauce/aromatics by weight. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries for these components. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries for these components. Trans fat is 0.0 as confirmed negligible in both shrimp and mung bean noodle components per USDA data. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Premium Vannamei Vermicelli' to the canonical 'Vannamei Prawn Vermicelli' to remove promotional language. The dish is classified as Thai cuisine based on input cuisine tags and the restaurant context (Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay, Singapore). Thai mandatory local-language name (thai script) is present. Sodium estimate of 380 mg per 100 g reflects typical sauce-seasoned preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation.
